Why the switch back to Cython 0.24.1 from 0.25.2?

I've looked into the matter and it seems to be largely a Python3 associated problem.

You might try to change the includes in `medpy/graphcut/__init__.py` to `import .energy_label.py`. But I won't promise anything.

MedPy has been designed for Python2, not for for Python3. Most parts should be portable, but some are not. There are many issues connected with the version upgrade.

During my testing right now, I e.g. figured out that the compilation of the maxflow module is done with libboost_python2 instead of libboost_python3 (whyever). Hence even if you manage to fix the import, it might not work!

I would suggest that you switch your anaconda to python2.7 (which should be easy, according to their page) and then install MedPy (whichever version you want) again. This will hopefully solve all your problems.

Please try again, I accidently only fixed the same problem with OpenGL contexts.

`max_loaded_major/max_loaded_minor` are the minimum of the GL/GLES context version and the supported version by glad. So if you get a 3.0 context but glad only supports 2.0 it will only use 2.0 versions (unlike before where it tried to use 3.0 functions which werent loaded). 

I thought about clamping GLVersion instead of introducing `max_loaded_major/max_loaded_minor` but I didn't like that.

```python
def boltzmann(T, V, *args, **kwargs):
    kbT = kb * T
    return exp(-V(*args, **kwargs)/kbT)

def periodic(x, x0, k, n):
    return k*(1+cos(n*x - x0))

distribution = functools.partial(boltzmann, T=298, V=periodic)
x = Variable()
y = Variable()
x0 = Parameter()
k = Parameter()
n = Parameter()
constraints = {Integer(n)}
model = Model({y: distribution(x=x, x0=x0, k=k, n=n)})
fit = Fit(model, ..., constraints=constraints)
```

Because bypy is a module with dependencies, and the dependencies might have not installed yet. In this case, the requests module.
The pip system (and ```install_requires``` field in setup.py) was introduced to solve the dependency problem, thus users don't need to fully read the installation manual and install dependencies one by one. Instead, one could just issue a "pip install <modulename>" and wait for its completion, unless some C headers (xxx-devel in RH and xxx-dev in Debian) are required and building failed.
If you create a clean environment (a fresh python + setuptools + pip, nothing else), you could observe ```pip install bypy``` failed because it tried to import module bypy, and bypy imports requests, and BOOM!, an exception poped out.

As the practice of you-get, they created an individual module for the version information, and this module have no dependencies, so it can be safely referred in both the main module and the setup.py script.
I will create a patch following the approach of this kind, when I have some time.

It was a design decision to handle missing OIDs this way.

To explain the reasoning behind this: The X.690 standard has a null-type. When accessing an inexisting OID on an SNMP agent, I could have chosen to return `None` instead of raising an exception. But then you could no longer distinguish between the case where the OID exists but returns an X.690 NULL value and the case where the OID does not exist.

In other words, if a call to `get` returns `None` you can't tell whether the SNMP-Agent really returned a NULL value, or you accessed an invalid OID and maybe want to warn the user.

I see no other proper way to handle this differently without causing bugs in the client code later down the line.

I suggest wrapping your call to `get` in a `try ... except ... block` like this:

    from puresnmp.exc import NoSuchOID
    try:
        result = get( ... )
    except NoSuchOID as exc:
        LOG.warning(exc)  # or something similar
        result = None

This way, you as the library end-user has full control over what happens when missing OIDs are encountered.

@pmakovec 

send_command() is a timing based it tries to figure out the proper amount of time to way for the command to complete and return the output.

send_command_expect() is a pattern based method. It sends a command down the channel and looks for a pattern in the output to determine it is done. By default, the pattern output is based on the trailing router prompt. 

Note, across time I have encountered a lot of issues with send_command() i.e. just using timing is very problematic given how devices behave/response.

Because of this, in Netmiko 1.0

send_command() will be pointed to send_command_expect(). In other words, send_command() and send_command_expect() will be the same thing
The old send_command will be renamed send_command_timing()

Seems like your archive database file somehow got corrupted, but I doubt gallery-dl itself is responsible for this. The only write operation it performs are simple INSERTs, one after another, and it handles concurrent access from multiple processes without problems (I tried to get a corrupted database like this, but to no avail).

You could try to re-create your existing archive by dumping its contents and feeding it into a new one:
``` bash
$ sqlite3 archive.sqlite3 .dump | sqlite3 new_archive.sqlite3
```
or you could try any of the other methods mentioned in [this thread](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/18259692/how-to-recover-a-corrupt-sqlite3-database).

Also: using an archive is not necessary to avoid re-downloading stuff **if** the files in your filesystem still exist.

The place where the folder (`isbntools` or `.isbntools`) his created is a mixture of os system, python version and installer used (pip, easy_install, conda, ...), options passed to installer (local, user, ...) and if is inside a virtual environment or not, however there is a finite list of possibilities. So the setup file gives the order to create the folder and `isbntools` searches the different possible paths to find a suitable folder (but with some preferences defined, e.g., if the situation asks for `.isbntools` folder and there are a `isbntools`folder it will use the first if exists and the later otherwise). That explains the above behavior that you reported. However doesn't explain: 

1. why `.isbntools` wasn't created by the installer (no clue, usually is an os thing and I cannot do nothing... my advice is to install the package inside a virtual environment)
2. why the program searched for `isbntools` instead of `.isbntools`. For that I found a regression error introduced by a 'release' script in `isbntools-4.3.7`, and is now solved.

#!/usr/bin/env python
from __future__ import print_function
import pybedtools
import argparse
import os
import sys
import multiprocessing

def featuretype_filter(feature, featuretype):
   
    if feature[2] == featuretype:
        return True
    return False

def subset_featuretypes(featuretype):
    return g.filter(featuretype_filter, featuretype).saveas()


def count_reads_in_features(features):
    return features.intersect(abam=bam,
                             b=features.fn,
                             s=stranded,
                             bed=True,
                             stream=True).count()
def main():
    
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog=os.path.basename(sys.argv[0]),
                                 usage=__doc__)
    ap.add_argument('--gff', required=True,
                    help='GFF or GTF file containing annotations')
    ap.add_argument('--bam', required=True,
                    help='BAM file containing reads to be counted')
    ap.add_argument('--stranded', action='store_true',
                    help='Use strand-specific merging and overlap. '
                         'Default is to ignore strand')
    ap.add_argument('--no-parallel', dest='noparallel', action='store_true',
                    help='Disables parallel computation')
    ap.add_argument('-o', '--output',
                    help='Optional file to which results will be written; '
                         'default is stdout')
    ap.add_argument('-v', '--verbose', action='store_true',
                    help='Verbose (goes to stderr)')
    args = ap.parse_args()

    gff = args.gff
    bam = args.bam
    stranded = args.stranded
    parallel = not args.noparallel

    # Some GFF files have invalid entries -- like chromosomes with negative
    # coords or features of length = 0.  This line removes them and saves the
    # result in a tempfile
    g = pybedtools.BedTool(gff).remove_invalid().saveas()

    # Decide which version of map to use.  If parallel, we only need 3
    # processes.
    pool = multiprocessing.Pool(processes=3)

    # Get separate files for introns and exons in parallel (if specified)
    featuretypes = ('intron', 'exon')
    introns, exons = pool.map(subset_featuretypes, featuretypes)

    # Perform some genome algebra to get unique and shared regions
    exon_only = exons.subtract(introns).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()
    intron_only = introns.subtract(exons).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()
    intron_and_exon = exons\
            .intersect(introns).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()

    # Do intersections with BAM file in parallel
    features = (exon_only, intron_only, intron_and_exon)
    results = pool.map(count_reads_in_features, features)

    labels = ('      exon only:',
              '    intron only:',
              'intron and exon:')

    for label, reads in zip(labels, results):
        print('%s %s' % (label, reads))

    pybedtools.cleanup(verbose=False)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Found the root cause of the problem. The reason for the issue was that the session timeout was larger than the request timeout. Whenever we would restart a process, the previous process' session would live for too long relative to the request timeout, leading to the request timeout error.

When looking at the standard java based kafka client, there is actually an exception being thrown when the session timeout is larger than the request timeout, or when the fetch-max-wait is larger than the request timeout. See code [Here](https://github.com/apache/kafka/blob/trunk/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.java#L611).

I've created a pull request that throws an error if these constraints are violated #986 . Ran the tests manually through pytest tough, since tox stuff wasn't working for me. Send me any comments on it if needed.

Thanks

Well, the old pyodbc passed Python 2.7 strings to the database as-is, so if what was in memory matched what the DB expected (including UTF-8) it would work.  However, the format in memory is not normally UTF-8 and requires manual formatting.

The core issue for Python 2.7 was that there is no internally defined format - however it was formatted when you read it into memory was the format.

Now pyodbc has the concept of an encoding for both `str` and `unicode` objects and applies it automatically.  (I will probably add a fake "raw" encoding that passes as-is.)

3.1.2b6 version but it does in my 3.1.1 version.

My original code had no .encode('utf-8') part until I started tweaking 
it, this led to sending me the wrong code.

It's not a big issue to me if it's a bother to you, I just wanted to 
inform you about the incompatibility.

Robert

This can be resolved using the following logic from the IDD file::

    ! Object Description
    ! ------------------
    ! To define an object (a record with data), develop a key word that is unique
    ! Each data item to the object can be A (Alphanumeric string) or N (numeric)
    ! Number each A and N.  This will show how the data items will be put into the
    ! arrays that are passed to the Input Processor "Get" (GetObjectItem) routines.
    ! All alpha fields are limited to 100 characters.  Numeric fields should be
    ! valid numerics (can include such as 1.0E+05) and are placed into double
    ! precision variables.

This logic can be used to figure out if the field is a number (based on A or N), even if it does not have a type. 

An exception to this are fields that can be number or a string if it is autosizable. For example the value of such a field can be 5 or autosize, if it is an autosizable field

Then I'd see which of those are supported by the `Table` methods in `python-pptx` and see which ones weren't yet.

There are basically two categories of things that need fixing:

1. The object model on Python 3.2 is kinda broken, and this results in some trivial output differences in some place that I historically solved just by not caring.
2. Unicode literals. There is almost no solution to making string literals work sensibly across Python 2 and Python 3.2. unicode\_literals causes a bunch of problems, and not having unicode\_literals causes a bunch of different ones. 3.3 introduced u'' prefixed strings and this made life vastly better.

2 is what caused me to drop 3.2 support as a very bad cost : benefit analysis. I'd personally rather not support it, particularly given the low usage numbers. Do you have a particular use case in mind?

From the docs:

### Setting the VBA codenames


VBA macros generally refer to workbook and worksheet objects. If the VBA
codenames aren't specified then XlsxWriter will use the Excel defaults of
`ThisWorkbook` and `Sheet1`, `Sheet2` etc.

If the macro uses other codenames you can set them using the workbook and
worksheet `set_vba_name()` methods as follows::

```python
      workbook.set_vba_name('MyWorkbook')
      worksheet.set_vba_name('MySheet')

```
You can find the names that are used in the VBA editor or by unzipping the
`xlsm` file and grepping the files. The following shows how to do that using
[libxml's xmllint](http://xmlsoft.org/xmllint.html) to format the XML for
clarity::


```python
    $ unzip myfile.xlsm -d myfile
    $ xmllint --format `find myfile -name "*.xml" | xargs` | grep "Pr.*codeName"

      <workbookPr codeName="MyWorkbook" defaultThemeVersion="124226"/>
      <sheetPr codeName="MySheet"/>

```

**Note**:

   This step is particularly important for macros created with non-English
   versions of Excel.



### What to do if it doesn't work


As stated at the start of this section this feature is experimental. The
Xlsxwriter test suite contains several tests and there is a working example as
shown above. However, there is no guarantee that it will work in all
cases. Some effort may be required and some knowledge of VBA will certainly
help. If things don't work out here are some things to try:

1. Start with a simple macro file, ensure that it works and then add complexity.

2. Try to extract the macros from an Excel 2007 file. The method should work
   with macros from later versions (it was also tested with Excel 2010
   macros). However there may be features in the macro files of more recent
   version of Excel that aren't backward compatible.

3. Check the code names that macros use to refer to the workbook and
   worksheets (see the previous section above). In general VBA uses a code
   name of `ThisWorkbook` to refer to the current workbook and the sheet
   name (such as `Sheet1`) to refer to the worksheets. These are the
   defaults used by XlsxWriter. If the macro uses other names then you can
   specify these using the workbook and worksheet :func:`set_vba_name`
   methods::

```python
      workbook.set_vba_name('MyWorkbook')
      worksheet.set_vba_name('MySheet')

```

I don't think putting a watch on getFilteredCollection would have an impact on perfs (as long as you don't set the last flag to true) as you ask to watch for reference (a simple pointer in memory). Have you already tried ?

I consider having a stateful service as a bad practice because you make your data flow in different directions and expect the result to be propagated through the watches (and obviously it does not always work :) )

I would rather see my application

1) load a view
2) get the latest data from service
3) ask service to update
4) update local view 

1) load another view
2) get the latest data from service (should have the update perform by the first service)

etc

If you keep a stateful service you rely more on an publish/subscribe pattern
1) load a view 
2) give me the reference of data => tells me when data is updated (based on bindings and watches)
4) ask service to update the view
5) I should be notified the data as changed

It is a bit Flux architecture vs binding.

I think the second pattern is only relevant when you have different components(controllers) in the SAME view sharing the same data. 
Otherwise I find it much easier to rely on injection and resolve mechanism

Having the same issue again in another context i figured out the source of my problem. I declared the translation columns as JSONB. SQLA isn´t aware of changes in such columns.
[SQLA-Docs](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/rel_1_0/dialects/postgresql.html?highlight=jsonb#sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql.JSONB)
I changed the declaration to:
`Column(MutableDict.as_mutable(JSONB))`
This should make SQLA aware of the changes made to the values in the dict. In my case it works perfect.

The last commit introduces deploying to PyPI from Travis and AppVeyor. For Linux and OS X, only x64 wheels are provided, as available. The maintainer workflow that is supposed to work is:
```bash
vi setup.py  # Bump package version
git commit setup.py -m "bumped version to X.Y"
git tag X.Y

# This publishes the tag on GitHub, available from the branch-switcher drop-down.
# It also triggers the master-branch builds on CI services, and the two are
# configured to upload build source and binary packages to PyPI.
git push --tags
```
You need to configure `TWINE_USERNAME` and `TWINE_PASSWORD`:
* for AppVeyor in `.appveyor.yml` environment; encrypt them [here](https://ci.appveyor.com/tools/encrypt) beforehand.
* for Travis, you can do it in Travis project settings. Do [escape](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/encryption-keys#Note-on-escaping-certain-symbols) any special characters in your password. This is [secure](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/environment-variables/#Defining-Variables-in-Repository-Settings).

The procedure was tested working, with sample blobs uploaded on https://pypi.python.org/pypi/MulticoreTSNE. The wheels were only partially tested, but I think they might indeed work.

@etseidler I was finally able to get this PR to work for me. There was a small problem with `bytes` vs. `str`. I assume you were using Python 2 and because of that everything worked for you. I had to make a minor change for Python 3 to work as well.

After my testing, I came up with two potential problems.

1. The code in the `ZmqManager.zmq_listen()` constantly spins at a rate of 0.5s, instead of sleeping until there is something to read. The spinning is a problem in itself, since it causes some CPU usage while nothing is happening, but more concerning than that is the hardcoded 0.5 second sleep, which means that a message that arrives can be delayed for up to half a second until it is received. I think that `recv` call should block until there is a message, but unfortunately the blocking call appears to block eventlet. I wonder if this is the reason why eventlet offers its own green thread wrapper to zmq.

2. I find the little message broker that you proposed to not be as robust as I would like. Do you know of any zmq message broker project that can be recommended? Clearly most apps do not use a centralized message broker with zmq. I feel there must be a commonly accepted pattern for multi-publisher/multi-subscriber to work well together without having to rely on a custom written message repeater. Let me know if you can suggest anything, I haven't found anything yet, but I'll keep looking.

If I'm not mistaken, the only time django-compressor checks for changes to source files is when you have the precompiler listed in [`COMPRESS_CACHEABLE_PRECOMPILERS`](http://django-compressor.readthedocs.io/en/latest/settings/#django.conf.settings.COMPRESS_CACHEABLE_PRECOMPILERS) - and the docs tell you not to use this for SASS compilers, for exactly this reason.

The default behaviour without `COMPRESS_CACHEABLE_PRECOMPILERS` is to recompile everything on every request - which sounds inefficient, but I haven't found it to be a problem. (libsass is quite fast, and invoking it as a library rather than an external program helps to reduce overheads too.) In production, offline compression is the best way to go (although `COMPRESS_CACHEABLE_PRECOMPILERS` would probably work nicely there, as you won't be changing files between server restarts).

Either way, I think this is out of scope for django-libsass - django-libsass just follows django-compressor's precompiler API, and compiles whatever files django-compressor tells it to.

The error you are getting is a side effect of how QueryDict objects work in django. In the job retrigger endpoint you are retrieving the list of job ids from request.data, which retrieves only the last item if the value is a list. In your case the value returned was the string '10' and when you iterate over it you get a '1' and a '0'. The id of the jobs you are comparing in your test are 10 (expected) and 1 (actual). 
To obtain a list of values from a QueryDict you need to call its getlist() method (see [the QueryDict documentation](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/ref/request-response/#django.http.QueryDict.__getitem__)).

```
def from_json2(class_type, json_str):
    type_dict_str = to_json(class_type)
    json_type_dict = json.loads(type_dict_str)
    json_dict = json.loads(json_str)
    json_dict.update({"py/object": json_type_dict["py/type"]})
    return decode(json.dumps(json_dict))

I haven't tried it, but in theory Annoy works with out of core data. If you put it on an SSD drive then it probably could perform OK. I think you would experience a slowdown of say 10-100x.

One issue that comes to mind is that Annoy builds up the index in RAM and then writes it to disk – it would be more efficient to build it up directly to disk. That should actually be pretty easy to support.

Spinning disk would be ridiculously slow – probably 1000x slower at least.

More harder stuff you can do to optimize for out of core:
 - Partition the index (like @piskvorky said)
 - Don't store the vectors in the index, just the search structure
 - Support axis-aligned splits instead of arbitrary vectors – this way the splits will only take a few bytes rather than 4kB per split (with (1000 dim))

I also encourage you to do some sort of dimensionality reduction before putting the data into Annoy – even a simple SVD down to 100D would probably help tremendously.

Okay, after some discussion, this is the ideal use scenario for this:

```python
kv = thrift.load('keyvalue.thrift')

@tchannel.thrift.register(kv.KeyValue)
def getValue(request):
    key = request.body.key
    if ...:
        raise kv.KeyNotFoundException()
    return kv.Value(value)

request = Request(body=kv.KeyValue.getValue(key="foo"))
response1 = tchannel.thrift(request)
response2 = getValue(request)
assert response1 == response2
```

The change to make `tchannel.thrift(...)` accept just a `Request` object in addition to the current `**kwargs` it accepts is a different change. So the immediate concern is to get this instead:

```python
response1 = tchannel.thrift(kv.KeyValue.getValue(key="foo"))
response2 = getValue(Request(body=kv.KeyValue.getValue(key="foo")))
assert response1 == response2
```

This will require a change to thriftrw that exposes a reference to the `*_result` type on each `*_args` type.

Also, there are two scenarios in parsing `requirements.txt` which add the complexity into resolution of this issue:
1. parsing `requirements.txt` just to get structured data about all package dependencies (including optional and platform specific)
2. parsing `requirements.txt` to match requirements with specific current system

2 is actually two step process, which includes step 1. But because 2 is more often needed than 1, it is mixed up together and makes API hard.

Later versions of virtualenv come with newer versions of pip and setuptools bundled. However with any virtualenv activate, you can always reinstall an older pip or setuptools. `python -m pip install -U pip==<version> setuptools==<version>`.

I believe it is likely a difference in the setuptools/pip version you are using by default in the virtualenv, and not any difference within virtualenv code itself. I think the difference specifically here is that pip versions after version 7 will repackage the django installation into a wheel before installing, and wheels do not allow absolute path [`data_files`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/stable/1.3.x/setup.py#L83) arguments.

https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/distributing/#data-files

You can downgrade a virtualenv's pip to 6.x, by having that virtualenv activate and running `python -m pip install -U pip~=6.0`

Since django 1.3 is so old I don't believe there's much more support we can give.

@chetan51 please review

Space-wise, passing active indices instead of binary representations is more efficient in most HTM cases. Take the SP output as an example: 2048 columns with 40 active. If we store the active indices with 32 bits each then we use up 40*32=1280 bits. If we store the representation in binary then we need at least 2048 bits.

I also did an experiment where I was seeing if I could more efficiently look for overlap matches in a set of SDRs (as compared to our sparse matrix implementations). I stored binary representations and to check for overlap used 64 bit bitwise-AND and compiler-optimized population count (__builtin_popcount). Despite only needing a few operations for each block of 64 bits, this implementation was still slower than the sparse matrix that just stored the indices of the active bits.

So from what I have seen it seems more memory and CPU efficient to store active indices, not binary representations. I am definitely interested to see evidence to the contrary but from what I've seen so far it looks like passing around active indices rather than binary representations is the way to go.

On Invenio legay we had to implement something related in order to implement the concept of `earliest_date`, i.e. the earliest possible known date among all those available in the metadata.

Say that year of publication was 1983, but preprint date is 1983-03-23, then with a naïve algorithm one would consider as earliest date for the record 1983-01-01. However the most accurate thing we could actually say is indeed `1983-03-23` since it's compatible with the publication year unprecise information.

Sure, but how often does that accidentally show up on the screen?  The main problem with images is that `obj.drawImage(image=im)` returns the image, so if you don't assign it to something, it explodes the screen.  If you just want to see what something is, you can do `print(bdfinal)`, which uses the `str` rather than the `repr`, which is probably what you want in most cases.

I never looked into it, but I wonder if interactive python can be made to print the str version of things rather than the repr for unassigned return values...

> Isn't the danger of forgetting a relevant parameter just as pertinent when writing the __repr__ as the __hash__ or __eq__?

Yes.  But it's easier to test.  If you forget something, then `eval(repr(x))` won't come back equal to the original. 

It's harder to check that you got everything for `__hash__` or `__eq__` since the test is that two things that should be different don't accidentally come back as equal or have the same hash.  Two-object tests are harder to make comprehensive than one-object tests.

1 - The way that `slug_field` on SlugRelatedField, and `pk_field` on `PrimaryKeyRelatedField` are totaly different.
From docs.
>**slug_field** - The field on the target that should be used to represent it. This should be a field that uniquely identifies any given instance. For example, username. required.

> **pk_field** - Set to a field to control **serialization/deserialization** of the primary key's value. For example, **pk_field=UUIDField(format='hex')** would serialize a UUID primary key into its compact hex representation.

pk_field on PKRelatedField is used for **serialization**. Here It's crystal clear that we do serialize/deserialize the fields already.

[Here](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/relations.py#L410) You can see slug_field used to retrieve the object. That's why it's named as `SlugRelatedField` and does not user `pk` to retrieve the object.

2 -  
>In case of many=True, we don't create a lot of instances, we create only one, performance is better.

You do create a lot of instances.
 
Please see my comments about pk_field on 4.

3- This is a subjective topic. Both approaches can be used. I told u what would be mine.

4- `pk_field` is used in `to_internal_value` method. But we do not use it in `to_represantation`. Because we want to change the way it serialized. That's why `presentation_serializer` is required.

There is no point of using it in  `to_represantation`.

Again, as an FYI: You don't have to clean out the server before import, it will work around existing items. If an item already exists it will update the item if needed. 

**IMPORT TO DESTINATION AWX SERVER**
    1. Import credentials
         This is currently a known limitation, the API can't export secrets. Personally I wouldn't want it to; if the API could get at secrets it opens up an attack vector that I wouldn't want on my server. On the send command there is an option for --secret_management which will prompt you for required passwords. While I don't have the process documented yet, I could also envision running this with Ansible and having an ansible-vault with all of your passwords. After the export step you could merge the resulting JSON with the passwords from your vault and then pass that back into tower-cli send or writing out JSON with your passwords decrypted.

   2. Import Projects
          This manual step you had too take was a bug, it should be fixed with the new version. Please test and let us know.

   3. Import Notification Templates.
          This is the same as password, we can not export the secrets.

   4. Import Inventory
          The first manual step (removing hosts/groups) I will look into. I'll need to see if the API can distinguish between a group/host provided by a dynamic inventory vs a manually added asset.
          The second manual step was a bug, again it should be resolved, please test and let me know.
         
**POST IMPORT**

Le concept, le principe de base de `black` est justement de ne pas donner de possibilité de configuration ("non-compromising"). Si j'ai bien compris https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/installation_and_usage.html#usage il n'y a que trois possibilités de modifier le formatage:
1/ la longueur de la line préférée (88 par défaut)
2/ le formatage uniquement compatible py36 qui consiste à avoir des "trailing commas in function signatures and calls" (désactivé par défaut)
3/ ne pas normaliser les (double) guillemets des strings

Ni gofmt ni elm-format ne permettent la moindre configuration, avec l'argument fort qui est "Il est utile pour une communauté d'avoir un seul et même style. Et il est donc souhaitable que chaque projet adopte le même style que celui de la communauté. Et donc si un projet veut bénéficier des avantages de l'outil de formattage, il devra adopter le même style que celui de la communauté". Il me semble même qu'en GO il n'est pas possible de *ne pas* adopter gofmt.

A use case for m.add()

There is the need to re-insert a state after it finishes. 
In EVM you need to emulate 2 'transactions'. The first would run over the evm world (platform) and reach a final stage/instruction. Then without resetting the memory and storage or any other platform internal data you make the second transaction. (Which is like a function call).  One way to do this is to subscribe to 'will_terminate_state' and reinsert the state into the queue via a m.add() (and prevent the testcase generation via e.testcase=False) ... Like this.

    #Callbacks
    def terminate_transaction_callback(self, state, state_id, e):
            do_something_else(state)  #Apply the next function call/transaction
            self.add(state)  #Re add state to queue
            e.testcase = False   #prevent the testcase generation

All sizes seem correct when compared to system.getsizeof, one exception the size of the `__slots__` in the `Foo()` instance.

The additional 8 bytes (compared to the size of `SLOTS`) are due to the following assumption inside function `asizeof._dir2` [on line 433 ff](https://github.com/ActiveState/code/blob/master/recipes/Python/546530_Size_of_Python_objects_revised/recipe-546530.py) ... _assume `__slots__` tuple/list is holding the attr values_ ... meaning the instance-specific `__slots__` values are _assumed_ to be stored in a tuple (or list) and that's how `__slots__` are sized: as a separate tuple (or list) plus a reference to that tuple (or list).

Perhaps that assumption and/or `__slots__` sizing this way is incorrect and should be revisited.  Hope this helps.

Just to add, this also breaks `generate_signed_url` for objects with `/` characters.  The signed string contains `%2F` instead of `/`, so the signature doesn't match the one generated by the server.  

The problem seems to be this line: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/storage/google/cloud/storage/blob.py#L1579

The `safe=''` overrides the default behavior of `urllib.parse.quote`, which is to leave `/` characters alone.  If you remove that param it should fix the issue, although you might need to confirm that other callers aren't expecting slashes to be escaped.

So I’ve finally got some time to look at this issue, and at first it seemed fairly straight forward until further investigation. Essentially the --build, --no-build option modifies the “precedence” list and adds or removes the source package. The issue is that the pex `resolve` API does not expose the resolve_options as a pass-through param (which controls the "precedence" list). There are two options: 1.) Reproduce resolve in pants which would be hacky, 2.) make an upstream change in pex to allow the resolve API to take in resolve option/builder.

This is actually a really interesting question. It's making me think we should probably have a specific policy to describe the distinction between occupancies and subaddresses. (There may be one that I'm not currently aware of.)

@tanyaschlusser, I also couldn't find an explicit explanation of the difference between `SubaddressType` and `OccupancyType` in the docs you linked – according to the Standard, occupancy would appear to be a *subcategory* of subaddress. This is certainly confusing, especially because usaddress currently tends to mark the types of tokens that @BenGalewsky mentioned as `OccupancyTypes`. Occupancy is clearly our preferred category, at least at this point in usaddress' life. This may not follow the letter of the Standard (although again, I'm not an expert) but it seems to work fine in most cases.

There are two problems that arise from this setup, however. For one thing, we may not be in keeping with the Address Standard: if [page 74 of the URISA docs](http://www.urisa.org/clientuploads/directory/GMI/Professional%20Practice/Address%20Standard/AddressStandard_Approved_Apr11_02Content.pdf) is right, then `SubaddressType` may be the preferred designation for "Apartment, Suite, Room, Unit, Office" – all of which we now classify as `OccupancyType`. But the second (and more pressing, to me) problem is that addresses frequently contain two pairs of tokens that match our `OccupancyType` pattern (e.g. "Apartment A Floor 2", "Trailer 5 Lot 4") which raises an error with the `tag` method. To fix this problem, I've been training data to maintain the preference for `OccupancyType`, but to label additional physical specifications as `SubaddressTypes`; but depending on our occupancy/subaddress policy, it may make more sense to simply tweak `tag` to allow multiple OccupancyTypes. 

Curious to hear @fgregg weigh in on this.

Note this issue is related to need for some sort of tool for setting up Kolibri in a fully automated manner.

Richard said:

The simplest way would be to POST to this endpoint: https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/release-v0.6.x/kolibri/core/device/api.py#L17
With a payload of this shape: https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/release-v0.6.x/kolibri/core/device/serializers.py#L47
URL specified here: https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/release-v0.6.x/kolibri/core/device/api_urls.py


Confirmed the above API works and the following shell script can be used for provisioning:

```
export KOLIBRI_DEVICEPROVISION_FACILTY_NAME="Demo Facility"
export KOLIBRI_DEVICEPROVISION_PRESETS="formal" # other options "nonformal" "informal"
export KOLIBRI_DEVICEPROVISION_SUPERUSER_USERNAME="devowner"
export KOLIBRI_DEVICEPROVISION_SUPERUSER_PASSWORD="admin123"
# ('id', 'username', 'full_name', 'password', 'facility', 'roles')
export KOLIBRI_LANG="en"
export KOLIBRI_PORT=8000

generate_post_data()
{
  cat <<EOF
{
  "facility": {
    "name": "$KOLIBRI_DEVICEPROVISION_FACILTY_NAME"
  },
  "preset": "$KOLIBRI_DEVICEPROVISION_PRESETS",
  "superuser": {
    "username": "$KOLIBRI_DEVICEPROVISION_SUPERUSER_USERNAME",
    "password": "$KOLIBRI_DEVICEPROVISION_SUPERUSER_PASSWORD"
  },
  "language_id": "$KOLIBRI_LANG"
}
EOF
}

curl -i \
  -H "Content-Type:application/json" \
  --data "$(generate_post_data)" \
  -X POST \
  "http://localhost:$KOLIBRI_PORT/api/deviceprovision/"
```

Long term we can think about adding a management command analogous to:
```
{{kalite}} manage setup --username={{ kalite_admin_user }} --password={{ kalite_admin_password }} --noinput
```
but with facility/preset options...

> > Why not use requests instead of old and dusty urllib?
> I think requests uses urllib internally. Anyway, adding an unnecessary dependency is not a good idea, IMO.

Yes. urllib is a low-level module and requests is high-level. Thus the API is more appropriate for daily high-level usage (which is nearly everything). E.g. this code:
```python
request = build_opener()
request.addheaders = [('X-OTX-API-KEY', self.key)]
response = None
try:
    response = request.open(url)
except URLError as e:
    if e.code == 403:
        raise InvalidAPIKey("Invalid API Key")
    elif e.code == 400:
        raise BadRequest("Bad Request")
data = response.read().decode()
json_data = json.loads(data)
```
becomes this:
```python
request = requests.get(url, headers={'X-OTX-API-KEY': self.key})
if request.status_code == 403:
    raise InvalidAPIKey("Invalid API Key")
elif requests.status_code == 400:
    raise BadRequest("Bad Request")
json_data = response.json
```
Also, en-/decoding and **certificate checking** is automatically done by the library. Otherwise, you have to do it yourself.

requests is installed on most Python installations anyway.

Right now tabulator receives all parameters from the dialect (see here: https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-py/blob/master/datapackage/resource.py#L280), so right now any data package with a valid CSV dialect fails to parse.

So we have to add special treatment for all implementations to remove `lineterminator`, which is something that I would prefer to avoid, or allow setting `lineterminator` in tabulator. I prefer to do it tabulator as it's more cleaner and future proof: Python might ignore this setting now but it may change in the future (as per the docs). Also, important is that it's currently ignored _only_ when reading, but not when writing CSVs...

Hi all,

I think one of the problem is tha youare iterating through the columns of the dataframe. When you have a lot of ime series and extract a lot of features you get a huge number of columns and this iteration takes a lot of time...

For example, using the tsfresh.utilities.dataframe_functions.get_range_values_per_column function on a dataframe with 200 columns and 60 indeces I get an execution time for this function of 329ms (best of 3 execution on a total of 100 with %timeit).

I wrote an alternative function working on the numpy array directly:
```
 def get_range_values_per_column(df):
    """
    Retrieves the finite max, min and mean values per column in df and stores them in three dictionaries, each mapping
    from column name to value. If a column does not contain finite value, 0 is stored instead.
    :param df: Dataframe
    :return: Dictionaries mapping column names to max, min, mean values
    """
    column = df.get_values()
    masked = np.ma.masked_invalid(column)
    
    columns = df.columns
    
    col_to_max     = dict( zip(columns, np.max(masked,axis=0) ) )
    col_to_min     = dict( zip(columns, np.min(masked,axis=0) ) )
    col_to_median  = dict( zip(columns, np.median(masked,axis=0) ) )
        
        
    return col_to_max, col_to_min, col_to_median
```

It get an execution time of 25ms (same measure). This function still needs to be more tested (it still gets a problem with median in some cases) and maybe improved but this could give the right direction for improving the performance.

For the record, I found this out, because I wanted to detect features on 60 EEG channels. The feature extraction was still usable but the impute function took way to long so I stopped it...

I messed with this in a VM and can confirm, this is not a `conda` issue but one of the launchers.

----

The `Anaconda Prompt` shortcut uses
```cmd
%windir%\System32\cmd.exe "/K" "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda 3\Scripts\activate.bat" "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda 3"
```
for which the last parameter is passed on to `activate.bat` as `"C:\ProgramData\Anaconda 3` (note the missing end quote).
If I change the target to
```cmd
%windir%\System32\cmd.exe /K ""C:\ProgramData\Anaconda 3\Scripts\activate.bat" "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda 3""
```
, everything works fine and `activate.bat`/`conda.bat` get `"C:\ProgramData\Anaconda 3"`.
Conclusion: For the `Anaconda Prompt` this is a `menuinst` (+`console_shortcut`) issue [quoting in `menuinst` is broken](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/menuinst/commit/fcf395dbc7ccfd5130b2c38a243056820034081f)...

----

What we'd actually need, is that in `console_shortcut`
```json
"scriptarguments": ["/K", "${ROOT_PREFIX}\\Scripts\\activate.bat", "${PREFIX}"],
```
should be changed to
```json
"scriptarguments": ["/K", "\"${ROOT_PREFIX}\\Scripts\\activate.bat\" \"${PREFIX}\""],
```
and in `menuinst.win32` *not* add quotes around `/K` via `menuinst.win32.quoted` in https://github.com/ContinuumIO/menuinst/blob/1.4.10/menuinst/win32.py#L292 and *not* let `menuinst.win32.quoted` unconditionally remove quotes at the beginning and end of the arguments in https://github.com/ContinuumIO/menuinst/blob/1.4.10/menuinst/win32.py#L292-L293, cc @mingwandroid. As previously discussed, it would be wise to add a bunch of test cases to `menuinst` to get all those subtleties right; breaking changes might likely have to be postponed for a `menuinst 2.x`, I think.

----

There is a conda package for `libtiff`, and when `pylibtiff` depends on `libtiff`, the library is always in a known location, i.e. `<PREFIX>/lib/libtiff.so`.  So there should not be the need to search for it.  Trust me, you don't want to set `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` or `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH`.  Setting these will cause hard to debug issues in other packages down the road.

def vw_fix_cygwin_path(in_path):
        """
        Trick to fix dirty path issues with Cygwin
        """
	#VW
	print ("VW: " + in_path)
	# vw_modified_cygpath = "C:/cygwin64/home/srini" + self._sim_cfg_file_name[11:]
	vw_path_cmd = "cygpath -w " + in_path
	vw_modified_cygpath = subprocess.check_output(vw_path_cmd, shell=True)
	print ("VW: " + vw_modified_cygpath)
        vw_modified_cygpath = vw_modified_cygpath.replace('\\', '/')
	print ("VW: " + vw_modified_cygpath)

```
$ az keyvault secret show -h

Command
    az keyvault secret show : Get a specified secret from a given key vault.
        The GET operation is applicable to any secret stored in Azure Key Vault. This operation
        requires the secrets/get permission.
Arguments

Id Arguments
    --id           : Id of the secret.  If specified all other 'Id' arguments should be omitted.
    --name -n      : Name of the secret. Required if --id is not specified.
    --vault-name   : Name of the key vault. Required if --id is not specified.
    --version -v   : The secret version. If omitted, uses the latest version.

Global Arguments
    --debug        : Increase logging verbosity to show all debug logs.
    --help -h      : Show this help message and exit.
    --output -o    : Output format.  Allowed values: json, jsonc, table, tsv, yaml.  Default: json.
    --query        : JMESPath query string. See http://jmespath.org/ for more information and
                     examples.
    --subscription : Name or ID of subscription. You can configure the default subscription using
                     `az account set -s NAME_OR_ID`.
    --verbose      : Increase logging verbosity. Use --debug for full debug logs.
```

These changes are currently available by installing the keyvault-preview cli extension.  You can find instructions on how to install the extension as well as file any issues encountered here: https://github.com/azure/azure-keyvault-cli-extension/

> Why must every integer in the range [min(X), max(X)] occur in the sequence?

The requirement directly follows from the definition of the Multinomial distribution (see Support on [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multinomial_distribution)). If your sample does not conform to this requirement, you could use [`LabelEncoder`](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.preprocessing.LabelEncoder.html) from scikit-learn to manually project it in the required range:

```python
>>> from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
>>> LabelEncoder().fit_transform([0, 1, 5, 10])
array([0, 1, 2, 3])
```

Notes from Day 1:

```rst
ipykernel:plotting


Plotting
--------
One major feature of the Jupyter notebook is the ability to display plots that 
are the output of running code cells. The IPython kernel is designed to work 
seamlessly with the matplotlib_ plotting library to provide this functionality.

To set this up, before any plotting is performed you must execute the
``%matplotlib``  :ref:`magic command <magics_explained>`. This performs the
necessary behind-the-scenes setup for IPython to work correctly hand in hand
with ``matplotlib``; it does *not*, however, actually execute any Python
``import`` commands, that is, no names are added to the namespace.

If the ``%matplotlib`` magic is called without an argument, the
output of a plotting command is displayed using the default ``matplotlib``
backend in a separate window. Alternatively, the backend can be explicitly
requested using, for example::

  %matplotlib gtk

A particularly interesting backend, provided by IPython, is the ``inline``
backend.  This is available only for the Jupyter Notebook and the
:ref:`IPython QtConsole <qtconsole>`.  It can be invoked as follows::

  %matplotlib inline

With this backend, the output of plotting commands is displayed *inline*
within the notebook, directly below the code cell that produced it. The
resulting plots will then also be stored in the notebook document.

.. seealso::

    `Plotting with Matplotlib`_  example notebook


ipykernel:install


IPython can be installed (different python versions, virtualenv or conda environments) can
be installed as a kernel by following these steps:

* make sure that the desired python installation is active (e.g. activate the environment)
  and ipython is installed
* run once ``ipython kernelspec install-self --user`` (or ``ipython2 ...`` or ``ipython3 ...``
  if you want to install specific python versions)

The last command installs a kernel spec file for the current python installation in
``~/.jupyter/kernels/``. Kernel spec files are JSON files, which can be viewed and changed with a
normal text editor.


nbconvert:index
nbformat:format_description
ipython:ipythonzmq

Indeed, the npm ecosystem is full of practises that make things hard for package managers. If you could avoid packages that do these sorts of practises, that would be helpful for me and others. If not, then we'll perpetually have to find workarounds. For example, packages:

- that split themselves into 30+ packages with 30 public version histories that maintainers need to track
- that use overly-complex buildsystems that really just boil down to a 5-line shell script
- whose build process involves downloading binary blobs off the internet
- that include binary blobs (e.g. prebuilt javascript) in their source repositories
- that depend on specifically-built versions of other packages not in the main npm repo
- that don't follow semantic versioning
- that do follow semantic versioning but change their major version every week so it's basically pointless
- that do follow semantic versioning but perpetually stay on version 0.x yet are depended-upon by many other packages. (Many of these could be very deep down in your dependency tree.)

This isn't good for users either, at least ones that care about using verifiably-open source code. We have to follow these standards when packaging things for Debian, unlike the npm ecosystem which enforces none of these standards. Yes, not having standards makes certain aspects of development very easy for developers, but you should consider other participants of the software ecosystem as well, when writing software.

Function scope annotations are ignored completely (so long as they're valid Python). With this PR, you'd get

```py
def test():
    a: fdsf = 1
    return a
print(test())
```

```
test.py:2: undefined name 'fdsf'
```

even though if you run the program, it prints `1`.

It probably makes sense to allow names used in function annotations to be imported without unused warnings, as explicitly importing `Any` from `typing` serves as documentation. But should it fail in the above case? I really don't know what the best practice is, but in a technical sense, this *is* a false positive.

Under f5/bigip/tm/asm/ I created a new file called violations.py with this following content. It is basically a copy of attack_types.py from the same directory, I only replaced the necessary names etc.

```
from f5.bigip.resource import AsmResource
from f5.bigip.resource import Collection
from f5.sdk_exception import UnsupportedOperation

class Violations_s(Collection):
    """BIG-IP ASM Violations collection."""
    def __init__(self, asm):
        super(Violations_s, self).__init__(asm)
        self._meta_data['object_has_stats'] = False
        self._meta_data['allowed_lazy_attributes'] = [Violation]
        self._meta_data['attribute_registry'] = {
            'tm:asm:violations:violationstate': Violation
		}

class Violation(AsmResource):
    """BIG-IP ASM Violation resource"""
    def __init__(self, violations_s):
        super(Violation, self).__init__(violations_s)
        self._meta_data['required_json_kind'] = 'tm:asm:violations:violationstate'

    def create(self, **kwargs):
        """Create is not supported for Violation resource
                :raises: UnsupportedOperation
        """
        raise UnsupportedOperation(
            "%s does not support the create method" % self.__class__.__name__
        )

    def delete(self, **kwargs):
        """Delete is not supported for Violation resource
                :raises: UnsupportedOperation
        """
        raise UnsupportedOperation(
            "%s does not support the delete method" % self.__class__.__name__
        )

    def modify(self, **kwargs):
        """Modify is not supported for Violation resource
                :raises: UnsupportedOperation
        """
        raise UnsupportedOperation(
            "%s does not support the modify method" % self.__class__.__name__
	)
```

Hi.  We don't support or test on RHEL 5, whose core operating system components date from 2006.  In particular, we only support Python 2.6+, and RHEL 5 systems ship with Python 2.4.  Virtualenv can be avoided if you know what you're doing, but other aspects of our pip packaging may not be natively supported in RHEL 5; we haven't looked closely.

Out main recommendation would be to upgrade to RHEL 7; the client is now included as part of EPEL 7 and works well there!  But if you really can't upgrade, you may have better luck with one of the minimalist clients for the Let's Encrypt service:

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/list-of-client-implementations/2103

In particular, I'd look at clients with very few dependencies, like [letsacme](https://github.com/neurobin/letsacme) or [letsencryptshell](https://mojzis.com/software/letsencryptshell/).  They aren't as featureful as the main python client, but they are much easier to run on very old systems.

Ah, actually I figured it out. If you [look here](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/__init__.py#L5), you'll see that `__version__` is always populated - it's populated with `'unknown'` if `setuptools_scm` has not done its magic, and it's populated with `._version.version` if `_version` exists.

Presumably what was happening before was that `readthedocs` was not running an install step, so `setuptools_scm` didn't run, and when it imported `__version__`, it got `unknown` rather than throwing an `ImportError`. So adding an install step fixed it.

The only remaining question is whether this can be made to work without the install step by changing the version import over to `pkg_resources.get_distribution().version` instead.

Alright, I figured out my issue, so here it is for anyone that might stumble on this.

In a lot of the marshmallow examples, they show class instances in the `fields.Nested` usage. This is where my problem was. If I switch out to just using the class name instead, then all tests pass successfully and context is specific to the individual usage.

It's subtle in the full example, it's:
`child = fields.Nested(ChildSchema())`

to this:
`child = fields.Nested(ChildSchema)`

Here's the full code with the fix. Hope this helps someone else.

```python
from marshmallow import fields, Schema, validates_schema, ValidationError


class ChildSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.Str()

    @validates_schema
    def validate_context(self, data):
        if self.context.get('foo') is not None:
            raise ValidationError('foo is set')


class ParentSchema(Schema):
    child = fields.Nested(ChildSchema)


class TestParentSchema():
    def test_with_context(self):
        schema_with_context = ParentSchema(context={'foo': 'bar'})

        result = schema_with_context.validate({'child': {'name': 'Joe'}})

        assert {'child': {'_schema': ['foo is set']}} == result

    def test_without_context(self):
        schema_without_context = ParentSchema()

        result = schema_without_context.validate({'child': {'name': 'Joe'}})

        assert {} == result
```

> So what shall be the outputs?
>
> a dataframe with the raw data?

I don't know what you mean by raw data in this context. The columns are unchanged? 

> a dataframe with renamed columns to match pvlib convention?

Probably, though would be great if this was an option with a rename=True default. Not sure if we should also change the existing tmy readers to be more consistent.

> a metadata dictionary?

readtmy2 and readtmy3 return a tuple of metadata, data. Seems reasonable to me. Has anyone been unhappy with this in the past or wished that those readers did more?

> a location...

I strongly oppose this. I think it is essential that pvlib's class layer lives above pvlib's functional layer, though, I will take all the blame if the distinction or the reasons for it are unclear. Returning a Location object would ruin the distinction. As discussed elsewhere, you could instead add a ``Location.from_maccrad(metadata)`` method that does the job while retaining a clean separation among modules and between the class/functional layers. See ``Location.from_tmy`` for inspiration. ``Location.from_maccrad(file)`` could even be possible.

> a dataframe with renamed columns to match pvlib convention & localised index?

I think that an IO reader should return a dataframe that is localized to the format of the timestamps of the file and/or the timezone specific metadata of the file. That is: if the timestamps say MST or -0700 then the dataframe should be localized accordingly, or, if the timestamps are ambiguous but the metadata says e.g. tz=MST then the dataframe should be localized. pvlib should not guess at the localization of the data.

Fine with me if you want to add a ``Location.guess_tz`` or similar method to ``Location``. Within that method, you can import and run the optional library of your choosing or make a request to the google api.

>pvlib.iotools.util to include some tools
> optional tool to retrieve the timezone

I can see an argument for ``Location.guess_tz()`` with no arguments since it would look up its own ``self.latitude`` and ``self.longitude``. Otherwise, I think it's better for users to make their own call to a library that gets the tz with one simple line of code.

```json
...
"users": [

],
"somes": [
  {"id": 1, "foo": "bar"},
  {"id": 2, "foo": "baz"},
  {"id": 3, "foo": "qux"}
]
```

Or, in JSON API, you would have `"somes"` inside a sub-root `"includes"` hash.  Ember understands this by convention and will skip the request for `/somes?id[]=1&id=2&id=3` when they're included in that manner.

I want to collect all functions (represented by FuncDecl) appearing in an Expr and store them in a HashSet. It turns out that for a function "f", the HashSet will contain N FuncDecl instances of f, if f appears N times. I think it is more suitable for the HashSet to contain one FuncDecl instance for each function f, regardless of how many times f appears in the Expr. This is related to how equals() and hashCode() methods are implemented in FuncDecl. 
In the master branch of Z3, the equals() method of FuncDecl is implemented using "==" operator, and the hashCode() method returns super.hashCode().

Yes, please check the long discussion in #138. There have been proponents of "explicit is better than implicit" and proponents of making it easier / avoiding pitfalls for novices. >  Is that enough to identify a node in the CFG, or should the whole callstack frame be represented in it?

`CFGAccurate` is context-sensitive, however, as a design choice (mostly for performance), it only takes addresses of functions called and call sites into consideration. Therefore in `CFGAccurate`, it is enough to identify a `CFGNode` by `callstack_key, address, looping_times, simprocedure_name` without specifying the entire frame. You'll have to write your own analysis if you care about all content in each stack frames in CFG recovery.

If you are trying to disassembly thumb code, you need to pass in an odd address. There is no way to distinguish between arm and thumb code with just an address normally, since it's a separate processor flag. libVEX gets around this by using the lowest bit in the address to represent the thumb bit.

If you're using Lua scripts then headers are not handled automatically. From scrapy-splash readme (`meta['splash']['dont_send_headers']` docs):

> by default scrapy-splash passes request headers to Splash in 'headers' JSON POST field. For all render.xxx endpoints it means Scrapy header options are respected by default (http://splash.readthedocs.org/en/stable/api.html#arg-headers). In Lua scripts you can use headers argument of splash:go to apply the passed headers: splash:go{url, headers=splash.args.headers}.

Scroll to the last example in the https://github.com/scrapy-plugins/scrapy-splash#examples section - there is an example of how to make headers, cookies, etc. work with a Lua script.

The purpose of gather is to allow us to make N passes on an utterance for entity tagging (one per token), as opposed to doing an N-Gram expansion on the utterance (which would be N! complexity). I can't do much about this in the script as this is just the CIT convention (outlined here: http://cires1.colorado.edu/people/jones.craig/PMag_Formats.html) and it would probably break the script for other CIT files if I changed it. I assume these are not the actual temperature values because I've never seen anyone run a temperature step with on a decimal degree. Is it a naming system is it like the decimal IZZI system? (.0 is zero field, .1 is in field, .2 is PTRM, .3 is Tail check, etc.) If so what are the designations, and does it conflict with the above system? For anyone else who runs into this error on macOS, note that the system installed Python interpreter comes with an old version of **six**:

```
>>> import six
# /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Extras/lib/python/six.pyc matches /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Extras/lib/python/six.py
import six # precompiled from /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Extras/lib/python/six.pyc
```

You can upgrade Python using Homebrew, override the default search path, or live on the edge and delete these files.  I upgraded Python via Homebew.

More info [here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/29666702).

Hi @casper-gh,
After trying different methods with both certbot and letsencypt, the most reliable solution that I have managed to reproduce everytime and without getting any error messages, is as per below instructions:

After creating a certificate with letsencrypt on your first RPi:
1. Locate the folder where your certificate is being stored. This should be in /etc/letsencrypt/live/<your_domain> by default.

2. You should see 4 files: cert.pem, privkey.pem, chain.pem, and fullchain.pem. Copy these files to your second RPi. I would highly recommend using rsync with a method like -a to preserve symbolic links, and then copy the entirety of /etc/letsencrypt. Alternatively you can create an archive with tar, again to preserve all the relevantsymbolic links.

3. Install letsencrypt on your second RPi:
[code]git clone https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt[/code]
4.Then:
[code]cd letsencrypt[/code]
5. Then run the command to re-install your certificate:
[code]./letsencrypt-auto install --apache --cert-path <cert_path> --key-path <key_path> --chain-path <chain_path> --fullchain-path <fullchain_path>[/code]
6. Make sure you change the ssl port on your second RPi by editing:
[code]sudo nano /etc/apache2/ports.conf
sudo nano /etc/apache2/sites-available/default-ssl.conf
sudo nano /etc/apache2/sites-available/000-default-le-ssl.conf
sudo service apache2 restart
sudo systemctl daemon-reload[/code]
7. Portforward the new ssl port from your router.

Hi @herrold ,

That colon is meant in a grammatical sense, like

> There's a problem with item 1235142: it's too small!

In this case, the item number is "`1235142`", not "`1235142:`". However, since URLs can also contain colons in some circumstances, I can see where this might be confusing.

This error message is created by the Let's Encrypt CA software, Boulder, not by Certbot. In order to change it, it would have to be changed on the CA side. (Certbot is just showing the exact detail string that was received from the CA, which already includes the colon.)

You can post an issue at https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/issues asking for the syntax of the error to be changed. Again, the developers there would have the power to change this, but we wouldn't.

Thanks, and sorry for the inconvenience!

I can also confirm from looking at the error messages that the specific reason for the failure of your certificate issuance in this case was not related to the colon and probably relates to your domain or server configuration in some way. If you continue to have trouble getting your certificate, you could try using the new third-party tool at https://letsdebug.net/, or post a message on our community forum at https://community.letsencrypt.org/.

I think the better way to do this would be to use nss_ldap + pam_ldap to make all users on ldap 'automagically' have local accounts on the machine. That's what we do at wikimedia. http://www.tldp.org/HOWTO/archived/LDAP-Implementation-HOWTO/pamnss.html is a decent guide. This has the following advantages over creating local accounts here:

1. Deleting accounts on LDAP deletes them from the system as well
2. If you are just using paswords, you can use the default PAM authenticator and it will Just Work
3. You can put users in different groups on LDAP and that'll be reflected here as well
4. It sees a ton of use among many different organizations, so a lot of your questions are probably answered!

Can you think of any advantages that putting account creation in ldapauthenticator would have, over using this setup?

The remote task system is designed to be interactive. The tasks can be submitted in Jupyter, in a cell, to any cluster and super computer, and you can monitor the status of tasks easily, the files are also automatically synced between local and remote hosts, and a nice thing about this approach is that the Jupyter notebook is not blocked when the tasks are executed remotely.

Mediainfo in VLC? The bit rates as reported in the HLS playlists are not the actual bit rates, they are the average bit rate targets. While the target average bit rate may be 4500k or 3000k, they are variable and may not be reached.

You can look at the stream URLs (using `--stream-url`) to see which version is which, eg.
```shell
$ streamlink --stream-url "http://network.wwe.com/video/v1228654883" 720p_alt

$ streamlink --stream-url "http://network.wwe.com/video/v1228654883" 720p
http://hlsvod-akc.media.net.wwe.com/wwe/2017/03/02/1228654883_m3u8/3000/wwe_1228654883_3000k.m3u8
```

In this case `720p_alt` is `4500k` and `720p` is `3000k`. 

In the master playlist for this video (http://hlsvod-akc.media.net.wwe.com/wwe/2017/03/02/1228654883_m3u8/master_wired.m3u8) you can see that the bit rates are reported as `3295600` (`3295k`) and `4945600` (`4945k`), which also differ from `3000k` and `4500k. So, everything in `trio._core` *is* exposed as public API -- we actually have a test checking this. But it gets split up between `trio`, `trio.testing`, and `trio.hazmat`. And the things in `trio.hazmat` aren't supposed to be any less stable than the rest of trio, they're just lower-level: intended for implementing nice tools rather than being nice tools.

I want to be able to say things like: "in trio, `await` always means a checkpoint" (but in `trio.hazmat`, that's not true: `checkpoint_if_cancelled` and `cancel_shielded_checkpoint` aren't checkpoints, but rather tools for building checkpoints). And "in trio, we don't use callbacks" (but in `trio.hazmat`, that's not true: `wait_task_rescheduled` does). And "in trio, concurrency always uses a nursery object" (but in `trio.hazmat`, that's not true: `spawn_system_task` and `TrioToken.run_sync_soon` don't). And "in trio, our APIs try hard to be as friendly and easy to use as possible" (but that's not exactly true for `trio.hazmat` in the same way it is for the rest of the library). Also, `trio.hazmat` is where introspection and debugging stuff lives, things you can use to pry open the normal abstractions and look inside.

The rough analogy here is that `trio.hazmat` is something like the `os` module: normally in Python if you want to open a file you use `__builtins__.open`, which gives you a sensible high-level interface. But if you really need to access the raw APIs your operating system exposes, you can use `os.open`. Along with other wacky stuff like `os.setsid`, `os.posix_fadvise`, `os.sched_rr_get_interval`.

So... hmm. Some things it would be good to communicate to users about this submodule (whatever we call it):

* When you're first learning trio, you can ignore it (and lots of people may never need to learn it at all)

* If you're trying to extend trio or implement your own "primitives", you should look at it

* It's a real public API, in the sense that it's documented / supported / stable (or at least, no more unstable than the rest of the library)

* But you may need some expertise and care to use it correctly.

@linkerzhang your comments raise a lot of interesting questions and allow me to further answer your concerns and I invite everyone to comment:

1. > ONNX as a standard will keep as it is to support only NCHW.

    We need to be careful about what you mean. There are no operations supporting NHWC format in ONNX; however, that does not preclude models from supporting NHWC format input. In fact, you cannot preclude such use case as long as you have a **transpose** in the operator set because you can always transpose the input once and enter the NCHW world thereafter. Thus, unless you explicitly pattern match and prohibit these cases in the checker, there is no way to prevent a user from creating  ONNX **models** that accept NHWC input but uses NCHW **operators**. Thus it is my opinion that 
    > This means all ONNX models should be in NCHW format,

    does not hold true as an implication from your previous statement.

2. Regarding your question of how I intend to use this PR, my answer is that I want to use this information to store the initial axis semantic of the input tensor. Regardless of whether 1 is true or not, ONNX is a generic neural network format and thus I do not see sufficient justification to assume a NCHW input annotations for all neural network models. Someone may be using an RNN or even maybe simply layers of activated matrix multiplication, in which case the semantic may be unknown and verifier should not kick in at all.

3. From a programming language point of view (neural networks are just more advanced programs), not having axis of tensors explicitly typed is a slippery slope to let go obvious and statically detectable programming (neural network construction) bugs. Let me walk you through a concrete example to put you in context. In the program below, we assume a NCHW model input:

    ```
    input -> Transpose(input, perm=[0, 2, 1, 3]) -> AveragePool(input, ...)
    ```

    In this neural network, a user mistakenly constructed a program that transposes an NCHW input to a weird NHCW format and pass through spatial pooling that assumes a NCHW input format. As clearly a mistake as it is, no existing infrastructure will report an error to the user. This is should be deeply unnerving to programmers who rely heavily on type checking as an integral part of program correctness guarantee.
